title: John the Baptist and Jesus' Baptism
objective: Understand John's preparatory ministry and the significance of Jesus' baptism as the inauguration of his public ministry

required_passages.text: In those days John the Baptist came preaching in the wilderness of Judea, 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.' For this is he who was spoken of by the prophet Isaiah when he said, 'The voice of one crying in the wilderness: Prepare the way of the Lord; make his paths straight.' Now John wore a garment of camel's hair and a leather belt around his waist, and his food was locusts and wild honey. Then Jerusalem and all Judea and all the region about the Jordan were going out to him, and they were baptized by him in the river Jordan, confessing their sins. But when he saw many of the Pharisees and Sadducees coming to his baptism, he said to them, 'You brood of vipers! Who warned you to flee from the wrath to come? Bear fruit in keeping with repentance. And do not presume to say to yourselves, We have Abraham as our father, for I tell you, God is able from these stones to raise up children for Abraham. Even now the axe is laid to the root of the trees. Every tree therefore that does not bear good fruit is cut down and thrown into the fire. I baptize you with water for repentance, but he who is coming after me is mightier than I, whose sandals I am not worthy to carry. He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire. His winnowing fork is in his hand, and he will clear his threshing floor and gather his wheat into the barn, but the chaff he will burn with unquenchable fire.' Then Jesus came from Galilee to the Jordan to John, to be baptized by him. John would have prevented him, saying, 'I need to be baptized by you, and do you come to me?' But Jesus answered him, 'Let it be so now, for thus it is fitting for us to fulfill all righteousness.' Then he consented. And when Jesus was baptized, immediately he went up from the water, and behold, the heavens were opened to him, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove and coming to rest on him; and behold, a voice from heaven said, 'This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased.'

segments.audio_script: Welcome to lesson three. In our last lesson we saw Jesus born and brought safely out of Egypt, fulfilling prophecy at every step. Today we fast-forward to the launch of his public ministry. A prophet appears in the wilderness to prepare the way, and the voice of God himself will confirm what Matthew has been building toward from the first verse.

Matthew chapter 3, verses 1 through 17. In those days John the Baptist came preaching in the wilderness of Judea, Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand. For this is he who was spoken of by the prophet Isaiah when he said, The voice of one crying in the wilderness: Prepare the way of the Lord; make his paths straight. Now John wore a garment of camel's hair and a leather belt around his waist, and his food was locusts and wild honey. Then Jerusalem and all Judea and all the region about the Jordan were going out to him, and they were baptized by him in the river Jordan, confessing their sins. But when he saw many of the Pharisees and Sadducees coming to his baptism, he said to them, You brood of vipers! Who warned you to flee from the wrath to come? Bear fruit in keeping with repentance. And do not presume to say to yourselves, We have Abraham as our father, for I tell you, God is able from these stones to raise up children for Abraham. Even now the axe is laid to the root of the trees. Every tree therefore that does not bear good fruit is cut down and thrown into the fire. I baptize you with water for repentance, but he who is coming after me is mightier than I, whose sandals I am not worthy to carry. He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire. His winnowing fork is in his hand, and he will clear his threshing floor and gather his wheat into the barn, but the chaff he will burn with unquenchable fire. Then Jesus came from Galilee to the Jordan to John, to be baptized by him. John would have prevented him, saying, I need to be baptized by you, and do you come to me? But Jesus answered him, Let it be so now, for thus it is fitting for us to fulfill all righteousness. Then he consented. And when Jesus was baptized, immediately he went up from the water, and behold, the heavens were opened to him, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove and coming to rest on him; and behold, a voice from heaven said, This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased.

Israel had not heard a prophet's voice for roughly four hundred years. Then John emerged from the wilderness in the mold of Elijah — rough garment, austere diet, uncompromising message. His ministry was specifically predicted in Isaiah 40, where a herald prepares the way before the Lord. Baptism was not new to Jewish practice; Gentile converts to Judaism were immersed to mark their entry into Israel. What was radical was John calling Jewish people — including Pharisees and Sadducees — to undergo the same rite, implying they too stood in need of repentance before God.

Notice three things happening at Jesus' baptism. First, Jesus insists on being baptized despite John's objection. He is not confessing sin; he is identifying with sinful humanity and formally commissioning himself to the work of redemption. The phrase fulfill all righteousness means he will satisfy every covenant obligation on behalf of his people. Second, the Spirit descends as a dove — recalling the Spirit hovering over the waters in Genesis 1. A new creation is beginning. Third, the Father speaks: This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased. The language echoes Psalm 2, a royal coronation psalm, and Isaiah 42, the Servant of the Lord. Jesus is king and servant at once. All three persons of the Trinity are present and active in this single moment.

Two major themes emerge. The first is solidarity. Jesus had no sin, yet he stood in the river with sinners. His whole ministry will follow this pattern: eating with tax collectors, touching lepers, bearing the curse on the cross. The second theme is divine confirmation. At every stage Matthew has shown fulfillment through prophecy. Now God himself speaks to confirm what the text has argued. Before Jesus preaches a single sermon or performs a single miracle, the Father has already declared his approval. Our standing before God does not depend on our performance; it depends on the one the Father is well pleased with.

Review question: What prophet does Matthew say John the Baptist fulfilled, and what was John's central message? Answer: John fulfilled the prophecy of Isaiah. His central message was repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.
segments.correct_answer: Isaiah; repent for the kingdom of heaven is at hand
acceptable_variants: isaiah 40; repentance because the kingdom is near
john fulfilled isaiah and preached repentance
he fulfilled isaiah's prophecy and called people to repent
isaiah predicted him and he preached the kingdom of heaven is at hand

segments.audio_script: You have completed lesson three. The public ministry of Jesus has begun, confirmed by the Father's own voice. In our next lesson we will follow Jesus directly into the wilderness, where he will be tested by the devil for forty days. Well done today.

vocabulary.definition: Jesus' stated reason for accepting baptism — to satisfy every covenant obligation on behalf of sinners rather than to confess personal sin
A farming tool used to separate grain from chaff; John uses it as a metaphor for the coming judgment that will separate the repentant from the unrepentant
